coredata.dc:title: Determination of metronidazole by a flow-injection chemiluminescence method using ZnO-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Zheng-yu
Xiao, An
Lu, Hua
Liu, Zhen
Chen, Jian-qiu
coredata.dc:description: ZnO-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by the chemical oxidation of activated carbon followed by zinc acetate impregnation and precipitation. The ZnO-doped CQDs had a stable fluorescence and a high fluorescence quantum yield. The fluorescence of the ZnO-doped CQDs can be quenched by reduction with metronidazole, allowing a flow-injection chemiluminescence method to be used to determine the metronidazole content in tablets. Under optimum conditions, there was a good linear relation between the concentration of metronidazole and the intensity of chemiluminiscence. The linear concentration range examined was 7.5 × 10−4−2.5 × 10−8 g/mL with r = 0.9996 and a detection limit of 1.08×10−10 g/mL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Metronidazole
Flow-injection chemiluminescence
Determination

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst pubtype ref alllist content subj ssids
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2014 Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: NEW CARBON MATERIALS Volume 29, Issue 3, June 2014 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: New Carbon Materials, 2014, 29(3): 216â€“224. Received date: 23 January 2014; Revised date: 25 May 2014 *Corresponding author. E-mail: cjqercarbon@yeah.net CopyrightÂ©2014, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-5805(14)60136-0 RESEARCH PAPER Determination of metronidazole by a flow-injection chemiluminescence method using ZnO-doped carbon quantum dots Zheng-yu Yan 1,2 , An Xiao 1,2 , Hua Lu 1,2 , Zhen Liu 1,2 , Jian-qiu Chen 1,2, * 1 State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China; 2 Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China Abstract: ZnO-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by the chemical oxidation of activated carbon followed by zinc acetate impregnation and precipitation. The ZnO-doped CQDs had a stable fluorescence and a high fluorescence quantum yield. The fluorescence of the ZnO-doped CQDs can be quenched by reduction with metronidazole, allowing a flow-injection chemiluminescence method to be used to determine the metronidazole content in tablets. Under optimum conditions, there was a good linear relation between the concentration of metronidazole and the intensity of chemiluminiscence. The linear concentration range examined was 7.5 Â€ 10 -4 -2.5 Â€ 10 -8 g/mL with r = 0.9996 and a detection limit of 1.08Â€10 -10 g/mL. Key Words: Carbon quantum dots; Metronidazole; Flow-injection chemiluminescence; Determination 1 Introduction Quantum dots (QDs) have been applied widely in bio-labeling, biomedicine and optical devices owing to their good photoelectricity properties [1-7] . However, their potential toxicity may damage the environment and human health [8,9] . Developing new fluorescent nanomaterials having non-toxicity, environmental friendly and good optical performance were highly needed. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have great superiority of low toxicity, stable fluorescent performance, easy to be chemically modified, good compatibility with organic, inorganic and biological molecules to semi-conductive cadmium or silicon based QDs [10,11] . Therefore, CQDs have been widely used in drug analysis, bio-labeling and medical field. The synthesis of CQDs are usually including laser ablation method [11,12] , microwave-assisted method [13-15] , electrochemical oxidation method [16-18] , hydrothermal oxidation method [19] and chemical oxidation method [20-28] , etc. However, the application of CQDs prepared by these methods was limited extensively [29] due to their poor stability and low fluorescence quantum yield (QY). The chemical oxidation method doesn't require any special device, and the carbon source is diversifying, simple and easy to obtain. This study employed chemical oxidation method, using activated carbon as carbon source, the mixed solutions of H 2 O 2 and acetic acid as oxidizing agents. The synthesized CQDs were modified by zincoxide(ZnO) doping. The fluorescence property of the obtained ZnO-doped CQDs was investigated. Because the metronidazole could enhance the chemiluminescence of luminol-potassium permanganate (KMnO 4 ) system and quench the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, the ZnO-doped CQDs were applied in determining metronidazole tablets by a flow-injection chemiluminescence method. This method could realize online and automation analysis rapidly and easily [30-36] . 2 Experimental 2.1 Materials and Instrumentation Activated carbon from wood dust was made in the laboratory. Hydrogen peroxide with mass percent of 30%,acetic acid, N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF),zinc acetate, sodium hydroxide, polyethylene glycol (PEG 2000N ), luminol, KMnO 4 , and metronidazole were analytically pure and used without further purification. The determination of metronidazole tablets was carried out by a flow-injection chemiluminescence analyzer of IFFM â€“ E type(Xiâ€™An Remex analysis instrument Co.,Ltd.) at room temperature and the contrast test was conducted by a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, LKB2150 pump and linear UV-vis 200 UV-detector). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a 8400S-Spectrometer using KBr pellets(Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained from a JEOL JEM-1400 microscope (JEOL LTD, Japan) UVâ€“vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-752 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission Zheng-yu Yan et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2014, 29(3): 216â€“224 spectra(FL)were recorded on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer(Shimadzu, Japan). Leica DMIRB analysis was conducted on U-LH100HG. 2.2 Synthesis of CQDs 2.2.1 Preliminary synthesis of CQDs by a chemical oxidation method 0.3 g of sawdust-based activated carbon and the mixed oxidant solutions (35 mL of acetic acid and 70 mL of 30% hydrogen peroxide) were added into a three neck round bottomed flask of 250 mL. The mixture was treated under ultrasonication at 25 Â°C for30 min and heated under reflux at 100 Â°C for 12 h. Bright yellow CQDs showed a bright blue fluorescence under UV light of 365 nm in the solution obtained. Large impure particles and incompletely-reacted activated carbon were removed by filtering the initial synthetic CQDs solution. The solution was evaporated in a rotary evaporator to 2 mL at 60 Â°C, washed and extracted with ether (2Ã—20 mL).The extracts were rotary evaporated again and re-dissolved in 20mL of ultrapure water.The acidity of the solution was adjusted to pH =7.0 with NaOH. 2.2.2 ZnO doping of CQDs Suspension was obtained by evaporating above CQD solution to dryness and then adding 100 mL DMF under ultrasonication for 30 min. Zinc acetate (340 mg, 1.55 mmol) was added into the suspension with vigorous stirring. Then sodium hydroxide solution(0.625 mol, 2.5 mL) was added by drop.Precipitate was obtained after centrifugation of the suspension at 2 500 rpm for 5 min and washed with ultrapure water repeatedly. The ZnO- doped CQDs were obtained after the precipitate was annealed at 200 Â°Cfor 2 h. 2.3 Determination of metronidazole The flow paths of flow-injection chemiluminescence analysis device are shown in Fig. 1. Each pipe was charged with ultrapure water until the baseline stayed at zero. Then luminol, KMnO 4 , ultrapure water and ultrapure water were charged into the four pipelines as shown in Fig. 1 and the signal of the luminol-KMnO 4 system was recorded as I 1 . One of the ultrapure water pipeline was replaced with CQD solution after the baseline became stable, and the signal of the CQDs-luminol-KMnO 4 system was recorded as I 2 . Finally the other ultrapure water pipeline was changed into metronidazole sample solution and the signal of the metronidazole-CQDs-luminol-KMnO 4 system was recorded as I 3 . 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Synthetic optimization 3.1.1 Synthetic time 0.3 g of sawdust-based activated carbon and 150 mL of the mixed solutions (V HAc :V H2O2 = 35:70) were added to a three-necked flask and heated under reflux at 100 Â°C for different times from 1 to 12 h. 5mL reaction solution was removed from the flask at 1Ä‘2Ä‘4Ä‘6Ä‘8Ä‘10Ä‘12 h and adjusted to pH=7 with NaOH.The fluorescence emission spectra under exciting wavelength of 317 nm are shown in Fig. 2a. The results showed that the obtained solution turned to bright yellow with increasing reaction time. The fluorescenceintensity increased with reaction time before 10 h and decreases slightly afterwards. 3.1.2 Modifier and annealingtime CQDs doped with ZnO possessed a high fluorescence intensity and stability while CQDs modified with PEG 2000N was unstable. The reason might be that PEG 2000N could not be coated on the surface of CQDs firmly. However, the combination of ZnO and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs would remedy the surfacedefects of CQDs effectively. Therefore, ZnO was chosen to modify the CQDs in this study. Fig. 1 Flow path of flow-injection chemiluminescence device (P: peristaltic pump, V: reversing valve, F: flow cell, PMT: photomultiplier, HV: High Voltage; COM: computerÄŽ. Zheng-yu Yan et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2014, 29(3): 216â€“224 Fig. 2 Effect of synthetic time on fluorescenceintensity: (a)fluorescencespectra under different times and(b) samples under daylight and UV lamp. Annealing was the last step for ZnO doping. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs doped with ZnO under annealing time of 0.5, 1, 2, 2.5 h was investigated. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs increased with annealing time. Annealing for 2 h was chosen considering the operation convenience, good fluorescence intensity and peak shape. 3.2 Characterization of CQDs 3.2.1 TEM Fig. 3a shows the CQDs without ZnO doping and Fig. 3b the CQDs doped with ZnO under TEM. It was shown that the both CQDs before and after ZnO doping were spherical particles with uniform diameter. ZnO coated on the surface of the CQDs, which may minimize the surface defects of CQDs and increase their fluorescence quantum yield. The diameters of particles before and after ZnO doping are 5-8 and 10 nm, respectively. 3.2.2 Ultraviolet (UV) and fluorescence (FL) spectra of CQDs Fig. 4 shows the UV and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CQDs, It was indicated that CQDs had a wide and strong absorption peak at 280 nm in UV region, in good agreement with literatures [11,12, 14,17] . The FL emission peak was located at 435 nm after an optimum excitation at 317 nm.The fluorescence intensity of CQDs doped with ZnO (c) was higher than that without doping (b) and there was essentially no changes in emission wavelength. 3.2.3 Leica DMIRB figures of the CQDs A drop of CQD solution was placed on the glass slide and observed through the Leica DMIRB. Fig.5 shows that the CQDs could produce blue-green fluorescence and the dispersion was uniform. 3.2.4 Infrared spectroscopy(IR) of the CQDs During the synthesis of the CQDs, the oxidant of H 2 O 2 may impair the carbon skeleton structure of activated carbon, reduce size of the carbon particles and bring some oxidation groups,such as â€“COOH, â€“OH, to make the CQDs electronegative and hydrophilic [37,38] . Fig.6 shows that IR spectrum of a) activated carbon, b) the CQDs without ZnO doping and c) the CQDs doped with ZnO. The strong absorption peak of 1 702 cm -1 belonged to carbonyl groups while the wide absorption peak of 3 415 cm -1 belonged to hydroxyl group in the CQDs with and without ZnO doping. However, there was no such absorption peaks in activated carbon. The results indicated that a lot of carbonyl, hydroxyl groups were introduced in the activated carbon during oxidation,but there was no change in the peak position of CQDs by ZnO doping. After the CQDs doped with ZnO showed a sharper carbonyl peak and a narrower hydroxyl peak than the un-doped ones. Fig. 3 CQDs (a)without and (b)with ZnO doping. Zheng-yu Yan et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2014, 29(3): 216â€“224 Fig. 4 UV and fluorescence spectra of the CQDs. 3.3 Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs The fluorescence QY of the CQDs was tested using quinine sulfate solution (dissolved in 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid solution, QY=0.54) as a reference solution because its maximum emission wavelength was the same as the CQD solution. The fluorescence intensity ratio and absorbance ratio of the CQD and quinine sulfate solution under the same excitation wavelength (317 nm) were calculated as the following. Qu= Qs [As/Au][n u 2 /n s 2 ][Fu/Fs] Qu, Qs are the fluorescence QYs of CQDs and the reference solution, respectively; Au, As are the corresponding UV absorbance values; n u , n s are the corresponding refractive index; Fu, Fs are the corresponding fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence QY of the CQDs was about 26%, which was believed to be high enough in biomedicine application. 3.4 Optical properties of the CQDs The effect of light, temperature, pH, and long-term storage on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs were investigated. Comparison of the CQDs with and without ZnO doping were conducted in a light incubator (temperature of 25 Â°C, light intensity of 50.12 Âµmol/(m 2 Â·s -1 ) for 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 h by measuring their fluorescence intensities. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs doped with ZnO was higher than that without doping, and the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged after 12 h of continuous light incubation, which indicated that the CQDs could resist photobleaching.The high QY of the CQDs doped with ZnO was beneficial for the fluorescent labeling. The fluorescence intensity of the CQD solutionexcited at a wavelength of 317 nm was almost unchanged by increasing temperature from 25 to 50 Â°C for 30 min. Dissolving CQD solution in PBS buffer and adjusting the pH value to 2, 5, 7, 10, 12, and then measuring the fluorescence intensity of these solutions under the optimal excitation wavelength of 317 nm, the fluorescence intensity was highest at pH 7.0, below or above which fluorescence intensity would decrease. No significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of CQD solution (400 Âµg/mL)were found after the solution was placed in laboratory for 8 months, indicating that the CQDs doped with ZnO was stable. 3.5 Determination of metronidazole A flow-injection luminescence instrument (IFFM-E type) was used to examine the luminescence reaction kinetics curves of three chemiluminescence systems: a) luminol-KMnO 4, b) CQDs- luminol-KMnO 4 and c) metronidazole-CQDs-luminol- KMnO 4 . The obtained curves are shown in Fig. 7. The luminol-KMnO 4 system showed a weak luminescence intensity (I 1 ), and the luminescence intensity was enhanced after the CQDs were added(I 3 : curve c), indicating that the CQDs could enhance the luminescence intensity of luminol-KMnO 4 system significantly. However, the adding of metronidazole to the CQDs- luminol-KMnO 4 Fig. 5 Leica DMIRB figures of the CQDs (a)without and (b)with ZnO doping. Zheng-yu Yan et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2014, 29(3): 216â€“224 Fig. 6 IR spectra of (a)activated carbon, (b) the CQDs without ZnO doping and (c) the CQDs doped with ZnO. Fig. 7 Luminescence reaction kinetics curves of three chemiluminescence systems: (a)luminol-KMnO 4 , (b)CQDs-luminol-KMnO 4 and (c) metronidazole -CQDs-luminol- KMnO 4 . would reduce this luminescence intensity (I 2 : curve b).Thus metronidazole could be used as a quencher of this system and âˆ†I = I 3 Ä’I 2 was the reduced luminescence intensity value, by which the amount of metronidazole can be measured. 3.5.1 Optimization of experimental conditions The optimum concentration of luminol, KMnO 4 , sodium hydroxide, and CQDs were determined as follows. Photoluminescence intensity of luminol in range of 6.0Ã—10 -4 ~1.0Ã—10 -4 mol/L was tested as shown in Fig. 8a , which showed that 4.0Ã—10 -4 mol/L was the optimum concentration, at which the value of I 3 and âˆ†I reached the highest. As an oxidant of the reaction, KMnO 4 also had effect on photoluminescence intensity. Photoluminescence intensity versus KMnO 4 concentration of 1.0Ã—10 -6 ~ 2.0Ã—10 -5 mol/L is shown in Fig. 8b, which revealed that the optimum concentration,at which the âˆ†I was the highest, was 5.0Ã—10 -6 mol/L. The alkalinity of the reaction could be changed by adjusting the concentration of NaOH, which was used to dissolve luminal. The effect of NaOH concentration from 0.005 to 0.10 mol/L on the photoluminescence intensity was tested as shown in Fig. 8c, which showed that the âˆ†I value was the highest when the concentration was 0.01 mol/L. Obviously, CQDs could enhance the photoluminescence of thesystem, and CQD concentrations from 0.004 to 40 Âµg/mL was examined as shown in Fig. 8d, which revealed that the Fig. 8 Optimum concentrations of (a) lumino, (b) KMnO 4 , (c) sodium hydroxide and(d) CQDs. Zheng-yu Yan et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2014, 29(3): 216â€“224 Table 1 Recovery rate of Metronidazole tablets by IFFM-E and HPLC. photoluminescence intensity increased with the CQD concentration while a further increasing would be out of detection range.Therefore, 40 Âµg/mL would be the optimum concentration of CQDs. 3.5.2 Linear range and limit of detection (LOD) in comparison with HPLC in Chinese Pharmacopoeia(CH.P) Determining concentration of metronidazole was carried out under the following conditions: the concentrations of luminol, KMnO 4 , sodium hydroxide, CQDs were 4.0Ã—10 -4 mol/L, 5.0Ã—10 -6 mol/L, 0.01 mol/L and 40 Âµg/mL respectively. The linear regression equation was âˆ†I=30126 C + 259.64 and the concentration range of metronidazole was 7.5Ã—10 -4 j 2.5Ã—10 -8 g/mL with r=0.9996 and LOD=1.08Ã—10 -10 g/mL. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for six parallel determinations of metronidazole was 1.14%, proving that this method had a good reproducibility. The concentrations of metronidazole were determined by HPLC method with reference to Chinese Pharmacopoeia for comparison. The mobile phase was methanol: water=50:50; ODS column, flow rate was 1.0 mL/min, detection wavelength was 320nm and the injection volume was 20 ÂµL. The metronidazole concentrations of 20, 50, 100, 200, 300 Âµg/mL were used to evaluate the linear range in HPLC method under the above chromatography conditions. The linear regression equation of Y=48467x+2.1Ã—10 5 was calculated with concentration as x and peak area as Y. The concentration range of metronidazole was 10 -5 ~ 2Ã—10 -4 g/mL with r=0.9997 and LOD=1.5Ã—10 -9 g/mL. The RSD for six injecting of 100 Âµg/mL metronidazole solution was 0.67% . Obviously, the flow-injection chemiluminescence analysis had the advantages of a high sensitivity, a low LOD and a wide detection range, which makes it possible to analyze substances in trace amount, e.g. concentration of substances in plasma. 3.5.3 Analysis of the concentrations of metronidazole 10 tablets of commercially available metronidazole tablets (Huazhong Pharmaceutical co., Ltd, Standard: 0.2 g per tablet) were coating- removed and pulverized. 25 mg of the powder was weighted accurately and put into a 25 mL volumetric flask, into which ultrapure water was added to 25 mL dissolve metronidazole. The sample stock solution of 1 000 Âµg/mL was obtained after filtered with a 0.45 Âµm filter. The standard stock solution was prepared under the same method. Put 1 mL of the sample stock solution to three 10 mL volumetric flasks and add 0.8, 1.0 and 1.2 mL of the standard stock solution, then dilute them with water, thus 80%, 100% and 120% solution were obtained respectively. The determination of metronidazole was conducted under the above experimental method in comparison with the method of HPLC in CH.P. The recovery experiments were carried out at the same time. Table 1 shows the rate of recovery by the No. Sample (Âµg/mL) Added (Âµg/mL) Detected (Âµg/mL) Recovery (%) RSD (%) IFFM-E 1 100 80 184 105 3.2 2 100 80 184 105 3 100 80 178 97.5 4 100 100 198 98.0 5 100 100 197 97.0 6 100 100 202 102.0 7 100 120 222 101.7 8 100 120 217 97.5 9 100 120 219 99.2 HPLC 1 100 80 179.6 99.5 3.5 2 100 80 173.7 92.1 3 100 80 179.2 99 4 100 100 193.9 93.9 5 100 100 196.1 96.1 6 100 100 199.7 99.7 7 100 120 217.4 97.8 8 100 120 221.4 101.2 9 100 120 223.6 103 Zheng-yu Yan et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2014, 29(3): 216â€“224 Fig. 9 Static chemiluminescence kinetics curve and the fluorescence spectra.Ä�a:KMnO 4 +CQDs+H 2 O; b:Luminol+CQDs+H 2 O; c:Luminol+H 2 O+KMnO 4 ; d:Luminol+CQDs+KMnO 4 . 1:Metronidazole-CQDs-KMnO 4 -Luminol; 2:CQDs -KMnO 4 -LuminolÄŽ. flow-injection chemiluminescence instrument and HPLC in CH.P. The recovery rate of the former could reach to 97.0% ~ 105% with RSD 3.2% while the recovery rate of the latter was 92.1% ~ 103% with RSD 3.5%. 3.5.4 Mechanism Fig. 9 shows the static chemiluminescence kinetics curve of a) KMnO 4 +CQDs+H 2 O, b) Luminol+CQDs+H 2 O, c) Luminol+H 2 O+KMnO 4 and d)Luminol+CQDs+KMnO 4 and the fluorescence spectra of Metronidazole-CQDs-KMnO 4 -Luminol and CQDs -KMnO 4 -Luminol. Maximum emission wavelengths of the metronidazole-CQDs-KMnO 4 -Luminol and CQDs -KMnO 4 -Luminol system were similar to luminol-KMnO 4 system at 425 nm, which indicated that the excited acid radical ion of luminol was the luminophor in both chemiluminescence system. The excited acid radical ion of luminol returned to the ground state by releasing energy with chemiluminescence while CQDs could not produce chemiluminescence. Chemiluminescence phenomenon was not detected in KMnO 4 +CQDs+H 2 O(curve a) and luminol+CQDs+H 2 O (curve b) while luminol+H 2 O+KMnO 4 (curve c)showed weak chemiluminescence and the luminescent intensity of luminol+ CQDs + KMnO 4 (curve d) was far stronger than luminol+H 2 O+KMnO 4 (curve c), which further suggested that there was no chemiluminescence between CQDs and luminol or KMnO 4 while luminol was actually the luminophor and CQDs just enhanced the chemiluminescence intensity. According to the chemiluminescence kinetics curve with chemiluminescence spectra and literatures [39-41] ,the mechanism of this study was supposed as follows. Luminol was oxidized by KMnO 4 under alkaline condition to get its acid radical ion that could produce weak chemiluminescence during its return to the ground state (Fig. 9c); CQDs was oxidized to be a radical cation or oxide (R+Â·) by KMnO 4 , thus both CQDs and KMnO 4 could oxidize luminol acid radical ion to enhance the luminescence intensity. Metronidazole has a reducibility with hydroxyl, which could react with KMnO 4 to reduce KMnO 4 concentration, thus lead to the quenching of the luminescence intensity. 4 Conclusions Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by employing oxidative reflux in weak acid system using activated carbon as carbon source. The CQDs was further doped by ZnO to increase fluorescence. The ZnO-doped CQDs performed a high fluorescence QY(26%) and a good fluorescence stability. A chemiluminescence method to determine content of metronidazole in tablets was proposed on the basis that metronidazole could enhance the chemiluminiscence of luminol- KMnO 4 but quench the fluorescence of the ZnO-doped CQDs due to redox reaction the ZnO-doped CQDs with metronidazole. Under the optimum conditions, metronidazole had a good linear relation in the concentration range of 7.5 Ã—10 -4 Ä’ 2.5Ã—10 -8 g/mL with r=0.9996 and LOD=1.08Ã—10 -10 g/mL. Compared with the determination of metronidazole by HPLC method in CH.P, the chemiluminescence method using fluorescent ZnO-doped CQDs has a low LOD, a wide linearity range and a high sensitivity, which is especially suitable for the detection of medicine or trace substances in plasma. References [1] Mattoussi H, Mauro J M, Goldman E R, et al. Self-assembly of CdSe-ZnS quantum dot biocon- jugates using an Engineered recombinant protein [J]. Journal of the American Chemical Society, 2007, 129(4): 744-745. [19] Bourlinos A B, Stassinopoulos A, Anglos D, et al. Photoluminescent carbogenic dots [J]. Chemistry of Materials, 2008, 20: 4539-4541. [20] Zhang J C, Shen W Q, Pan D Y, et al. Controlled synthesis of green and blue luminescent carbon nanoparticles with high yields by the carbonization of sucrose [J]. New Journal of Chemistry, 2010, 34: 591-593. [21] Pan D Y, Zhang J C, Li Z, et al. Hydrothermal route for cutting grapheme sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots [J]. Advanced Materials, 2010, 22(6): 734-738. [22] Peng H, Travas-Sejdic J. Simple aqueous solution route to luminescent carbogenic dots from carbohydrates [J]. Chemistry of Materials, 2009, 21: 5563-5565. [23] Liu R L, Wu D Q, Liu S H, et al. An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbondots using silica spheres as carriers [J]. Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 2009, 48: 4598-4601. [24] [24] Tian L, Ghosh D, Chen W, et al. Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot [J]. Chemistry of Materials, 2009, 21: 2803-2809. [25] Ray S C, Saha A, Jana N R, et al. Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization and bioimaging application [J]. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C, 2009,113: 18546-18551. [26] Liu H, Ye T, Mao C D. Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot [J]. Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 2007,46(34): 6473-6475. [27] Bottini M, Balasubramanian C, Dawson M, et al. Isolation and characterization of fluorescent nanoparticles from pristine and oxidized electric arc-produced single-walled carbon nanotubes [J]. The Journal of Physical Chemistry B, 2006, 110(2): 831-836. [28] Xu X Y, Ray R, Gu Y L, et al. Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments [J]. The Journal of Physical Chemistry B, 2006, 110(2): 831-836. [28] Xu X Y, Ray R, Gu Y L, et al. Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon

item-info.ce:copyright: Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

head.ce:title: Determination of metronidazole by a flow-injection chemiluminescence method using ZnO-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ZnO-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by the chemical oxidation of activated carbon followed by zinc acetate impregnation and precipitation. The ZnO-doped CQDs had a stable fluorescence and a high fluorescence quantum yield. The fluorescence of the ZnO-doped CQDs can be quenched by reduction with metronidazole, allowing a flow-injection chemiluminescence method to be used to determine the metronidazole content in tablets. Under optimum conditions, there was a good linear relation between the concentration of metronidazole and the intensity of chemiluminiscence. The linear concentration range examined was 7.5 × 10−4−2.5 × 10−8 g/mL with r = 0.9996 and a detection limit of 1.08×10−10 g/mL.
